title: Integrity Against Accusations
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Integrity Against Accusations_ 2 Corinthians 1_15-2_4 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt

outline.title: Introduction: Paul Falsely Accused
subpoints: For years Paul poured himself into the Corinthian church — planted it, nurtured it, counseled it — and in return they accused him of dishonesty, writing obscure letters to deceive, and speaking from both sides of his mouth (yes-yes and no-no in the same conversation)
Paul demonstrates Proverbs 15:1 — a soft answer turns away wrath; he does not vindicate himself for the sake of self but to protect the integrity of the gospel

Three truths in this passage: a good conscience is the first line of defense against false accusations; a Christlike attitude is equally important; great love for the brethren goes a long way in refuting false accusations

outline.title: 1. A Good Conscience Is the First Line of Defense Against False Accusations
subpoints: Verse 12: Paul's conscience is clear — 'We conducted ourselves in the world in simplicity and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom but by the grace of God'
A clear conscience allows us to look past personal attacks and focus on the issues being raised — it also means we will not avoid difficult relationships out of false guilt
Verse 15: in confidence of a clear conscience he planned to come to them — he believes that being together with a clear conscience is a second work of grace in their lives

How to clear a troubled conscience: Psalm 51 — cling to God's mercy; confess specific sins; believe that God can completely cleanse you; God is the God of your salvation who is willing to cleanse from all unrighteousness

outline.title: 2. Truth in Love Marks Our Relationships
subpoints: Our relationships must be based on truth and based in love — Ephesians 4:14-15: communal growth in Christ is not the result of cunning craftiness but of speaking truth in love
John 8:32: 'The truth will make you free' — what makes our conscience free is being truthful people, specifically truth-in-love sorts of people
Psalm 24:3-5; Psalm 15: the one who may ascend the hill of the Lord has clean hands, a pure heart, who has not sworn deceitfully — these are the ones God blesses
Are your relationships characterized by truth in love — or by avoidance, deception, or manipulation?

outline.title: 3. A Christlike Attitude When Facing False Accusations
subpoints: Verses 19-22: Paul's defense is grounded in the faithfulness of Christ — Jesus is not yes and no; he is always yes; all the promises of God are yes and amen in him; so Paul, as a minister of the unchanging Christ, does not equivocate
The author of Hebrews says: 'Jesus is the same yesterday, today, and forever.' James says of the Father: 'There is no variation or shadow of turning in him' — Paul serves and reflects this God
Verses 21-22: Paul and his co-workers have been established, anointed, sealed, and given the Spirit as a guarantee — they are united to the Christ who does not equivocate
The category of a follower of Jesus Christ who is not true to his word does not exist in the Bible; Christians are to be people whose yes means yes and whose no means no

outline.title: 4. A Great Love for the Brethren Refutes False Accusations
subpoints: Chapter 2:1-4: whatever Paul did, he did out of love — the painful visit, the difficult letter, both were motivated by love for the Corinthians
Verse 24: 'Not that we have dominion over your faith, but we are your fellow workers for your joy' — Paul is not seeking power or building his own empire; his motive is their joy in Christ
Verse 4: 'Out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote to you with many tears — not that you should be grieved but that you might know the love which I have abundantly for you'
Speaking hard truth when motivated by genuine love: it was not easy or pleasant for Paul to address problems in Corinth, but he did it because he loved them; love perseveres in telling the truth

outline.title: Conclusion: Ten Ways to Apply This Passage
subpoints: Guard your conscience daily; speak plainly and truthfully in small things; resist the urge to retaliate when falsely accused; do not avoid difficult relationships if your conscience is clear
Be willing to call God as your witness — live such that God's knowledge of your heart comforts rather than convicts you; reflect the unchanging Christ in your reliability
Examine the motives behind your decisions; speak hard truths with genuine love — not as a 'truth teller' without love, but as a truth-in-love person for the good of others
Let love, not self-protection, shape your responses; rest in God's final judgment — you do not need to win every argument to be faithful

summary:
Preaching from 2 Corinthians 1:12-2:4, Pastor Tito Lyro addresses a situation every believer eventually faces: being falsely accused by people you have served sacrificially. Paul had poured himself into the Corinthian church for years — planting it, ministering to it, counseling it from Scripture — and in return was accused of dishonesty, deliberate obscurity, and double-talk. His response demonstrates Proverbs 15:1: a soft answer turns away wrath. He does not vindicate himself merely for his own reputation's sake but to protect the integrity of the gospel proclamation.

Three main truths structure the passage. First, a clear conscience is the first line of defense against false accusations — it allows a person to deal with accusers calmly and Godly, looking past personal attacks to the real issues. For those with a troubled conscience, the sermon turns to Psalm 51 as the path to cleansing: cling to God's mercy, confess specific sins, and believe that God is the God who cleanses from all unrighteousness. Second, a Christlike attitude is essential: Paul's defense is grounded in the character of the Christ he preaches — Jesus is not yes and no, he is always yes, and so Paul as Christ's minister is committed to the same integrity. Third, genuine love for the brethren goes a long way: both the difficult visit and the tearful letter were motivated not by power or self-protection but by love for the Corinthians' joy in Christ.

The sermon closes with ten practical applications: guard your conscience daily; speak plainly in small things; resist retaliation; do not avoid difficult relationships; be willing to call God as witness; reflect Christ's reliability; examine motives; speak truth in love; let love not self-protection govern responses; and rest in God's final judgment rather than needing to win every argument.
transcript:
## Introduction: Falsely Accused

Have you ever been accused of something you absolutely did not do? When that happens, there is more hurt in it than when you are accused of something you actually did. Imagine the pain of giving yourself, of spending yourself for someone, just to have them accuse you of not loving them.

This is exactly where Paul is in 2 Corinthians. For years he poured himself into the Corinthian church. He planted that church. He ministered to them. He preached the Word of God faithfully. He counseled them from the Scriptures. He ministered to their spiritual needs. And in return, they accused him of three things: being dishonest, writing letters that were hard to understand in order to deceive, and speaking from both sides of his mouth — saying yes and no about the same things in the same conversation.

We are going to read 2 Corinthians 1:12 through 2:4:

*For our boasting is this: the testimony of our conscience that we conducted ourselves in the world in simplicity and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom but by the grace of God, and more abundantly toward you. For we are not writing any other things to you than what you read or understand. Now I trust you will understand, even to the end — as also you have understood us in part — that we are your boast as you also are ours in the day of the Lord Jesus. And in this confidence I intended to come to you before, that you might have a second benefit — to pass by way of you to Macedonia, to come again from Macedonia to you, and be helped by you on my way to Judea. Therefore, when I was planning this, did I do it lightly? Or the things I plan, do I plan according to the flesh, that with me there should be yes, yes, and no, no? But as God is faithful, our word to you was not yes and no. For the Son of God, Jesus Christ, who was preached among you by us — by me, Silvanus, and Timothy — was not yes and no, but in him was yes. For all the promises of God in him are yes, and in him amen, to the glory of God through us. Now he who establishes us with you in Christ and has anointed us is God, who also has sealed us and given us the Spirit in our hearts as a guarantee. Moreover I call God as witness against my soul, that to spare you I came no more to Corinth. Not that we have dominion over your faith, but are fellow workers for your joy; for by faith you stand. But I determined this within myself, that I would not come again to you in sorrow. For if I make you sorrowful, then who is he who makes me glad but the one who is made sorrowful by me? And I wrote this very thing to you, lest, when I came, I should have sorrow over those from whom I ought to have joy, having confidence in you all that my joy is the joy of you all. For out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote to you, with many tears, not that you should be grieved, but that you might know the love which I have so abundantly for you.*

Paul is demonstrating in the way he responds to the Corinthian accusations the truth of Proverbs 15:1: a soft answer turns away wrath. He could have decided to vindicate himself and accuse them back. But he doesn't do that. He doesn't try to defend himself just for the sake of defending himself. He answers their accusations specifically because what matters to him is that the gospel can be understood. He is defending the integrity of the gospel.

At least three truths emerge from what Paul says here. The first is that a good conscience is the first line of defense against false accusations. The second is that a Christlike attitude is equally important when facing false accusations. And the third is that a great love for the brethren goes a long way in refuting false accusations.

## 1. A Good Conscience Is the First Line of Defense

A good conscience is the first line of defense against false accusations. As hurtful as it is to be falsely accused, having a clear conscience allows us to deal with those who accuse us in a calm and Godly way. A lot of conflicts happen because people do not have a clear conscience. But a clear conscience allows us to look past personal attacks and focus on the issues being raised.

Paul's conscience is clear because he was truthful and honest with the Corinthian church. What they read in his letters is what he meant. There is no hidden agenda. He is not trying to say something over here and something different over there.

In doing that, he sets a pattern for how we are to behave toward one another. Our relationships must be based on truth and based in love. Ephesians 4:14-15: 'We should no longer be children tossed to and fro and carried about with every wind of doctrine, by the trickery of men, in the cunning craftiness of deceitful plotting — but speaking the truth in love, may grow up in all things into him who is the head — Christ.' Communal growth in Christ is not the result of cunning craftiness. It is the result of truth in love.

Is this what characterizes your relationships? Are you able to relate to others with a clear conscience because you are a truth-in-love sort of person?

In John 8:32, Jesus says: 'You shall know the truth and the truth shall make you free.' What makes our conscience free is being truthful people — but not just truthful, truth-in-love sorts of people. The Lord blesses those who have a clear conscience. Psalm 24: 'Who may ascend into the hill of the Lord? He who has clean hands and a pure heart, who has not lifted up his soul to an idol, nor sworn deceitfully. He shall receive blessing from the Lord.'

Because Paul has a clear conscience, he was not going to avoid the Corinthian Christians. In verse 15 he says that in this confidence of a clear conscience he planned to come to them. He believes that being together — with a clear conscience and a reconciled relationship — is a second work of grace in their lives. We often avoid people because we do not have a clear conscience.

Now one of the accusations brought against Paul was that he says yes when convenient and no when convenient — even on the very same issue. That is the idea of yes-yes and no-no: speaking from both sides of his mouth. Paul responds in verse 16 that he really meant to go to Corinth and made concrete plans to do so. In verse 17 he asks them several questions — questions that all require a negative answer. 'You know me. You have seen how I have behaved in your presence. You have known me for years. Do you really have evidence that I am a person who speaks from both sides of his mouth?' The grammatical answer is: no, we do not.

Notice that Paul is so sure of his innocence that twice he takes an oath before God — in verse 18 and in verse 23. The Bible does not forbid oaths. The point we should see is that God himself is a witness to the fact that Paul has conducted himself above reproach in his dealings with the Corinthians.

Is our conscience so clear regarding our relationships that we can call God as a witness to it? Are we brave enough to do that? God already knows our hearts — even if we can fool everyone around us, we cannot fool him. Hebrews 4:13: 'There is no creature hidden from his sight, but all things are naked and open to the eyes of him to whom we must give an account.'

If you do not have a clear conscience before God, the only way to clear it is to repent before God. Look at Psalm 51. David does not have a clear conscience — he has sinned against God, against Bathsheba, against Uriah, against the entire nation of Israel. So he says in verse 1: 'Have mercy upon me, O God, according to your lovingkindness. According to the multitude of your tender mercies, blot out my transgressions.' You have a dirty conscience? Cling to the mercy and tenderness of God. He will forgive you.

Verse 2: 'Wash me thoroughly from my iniquity and cleanse me from my sin.' Only God can clear your conscience.

Verse 3: 'I acknowledge my transgressions and my sin is always before me.' Acknowledge specific sins. If you think you are a great sinner but cannot identify sin in your life, you do not really think of yourself as a sinner.

Verse 4: 'Against you, you only, have I sinned and done this evil in your sight.' Do you believe that your sin is evil against God? Not just a mistake, not just a lack of wisdom, not just a slip — but evil in the sight of God.

Verse 7: 'Purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean; wash me and I shall be whiter than snow.' Do you believe that God can completely cleanse you from the sins you are harboring in your heart?

Verse 14: 'Deliver me from the guilt of blood, O God, the God of my salvation, and my tongue shall sing aloud of your righteousness.' Is this your God? The God of your salvation who is willing to cleanse you from all your sins? The gift of a clear conscience is wonderful — but many of us are not there, and we need to have our conscience cleared. Go to the Lord, confess your sins, and he will cleanse you from all unrighteousness.

## 2. A Christlike Attitude When Facing False Accusations

The second truth is that a Christlike attitude is equally important when facing false accusations. In verse 19, Paul says: 'For the Son of God, Jesus Christ, who was preached among you by us — was not yes and no, but in him was yes. For all the promises of God in him are yes, and in him amen, to the glory of God through us.'

Paul says: Jesus doesn't equivocate. In verse 19: 'I am the minister of Christ, therefore I do not equivocate.' The author of Hebrews says that Jesus is the same yesterday, today, and forever. James says that the Father has no variation or shadow of turning. It was this Jesus — the unchanging, truthful God — that Paul, Silas, and Timothy preached to the Corinthians. And in verse 20, Paul says this Jesus is truth himself — in whom everything God has ever promised is guaranteed.

This is Paul's logic in his argument with the Corinthians. He says: we serve and preach Christ, who does not change, who does not vacillate, who sticks to what he says. And because that is the Lord we serve, we are like him. So in verses 21-22, Paul, Silas, and Timothy have been established, anointed, sealed, and given the Spirit as a guarantee — and as followers of the one who is Truth, they are truthful people as well.

The category of a follower of Jesus Christ who is not true to his word does not exist in the Bible. Those Christians exist — but they are in a dangerous place, because the Bible says a Christian, a follower of Jesus Christ, is true to his word. Psalm 15: 'Who may abide in your tabernacle?' One of the answers is: 'He who swears to his own hurt and does not change.' Our Savior himself says: 'Let your yes be yes and your no be no.'

So let me ask you: are your relationships marked by truthfulness that reflects the Lord Jesus Christ in your life? Are you a person of your word? Can people trust your word?

The reason for the change of plans Paul mentions — not coming to Corinth — was not fickleness on his part. In verse 23, he says he stayed away to spare them. He wanted their joy — implied in Christ. He was not staying away to be mean or to avoid them; he was staying away because he wanted them to grow in the joy of the Lord.

## 3. Great Love for the Brethren Refutes False Accusations

In chapter 2:1-4, Paul expresses that whatever he did, he did out of love for the Corinthians.

Paul had visited Corinth on a painful visit — one we do not have recorded — to deal with a discipline issue. Some in the church did not receive it well. We see that implied in verse 1. He also wrote a letter dealing with the same issue — a letter we do not have — to which the Corinthians took offense. We see that in verse 3 and in chapter 7.

Paul's intent was not to offend but to bless. His motivation in addressing the problems in Corinth was love — not power-building of any sort. In verse 24, he says he is not there to lord it over them. He says: 'We are fellow workers for your joy.' And in verse 4: 'For out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote to you, with many tears — not that you should be grieved, but that you might know the love which I have so abundantly for you.'

It was not easy or pleasant to say and to write what they needed to hear. But Paul did it because he loved them. He persevered in telling them what they needed to hear because love perseveres in truth.

## Conclusion: Ten Ways to Live This Out

How shall we be people of good conscience, Christlike in our responses, acting in love despite false accusations? Let me suggest ten ways in which what we have heard this morning should shape the way we live. I challenge you to go over the text again and find even more.

**One:** Guard your conscience daily — not just when trouble comes. Ask yourself regularly: was I honest today in my words, my motives, and my actions? Confess quickly to the Lord. He will take care of it. A clear conscience will not stop false accusations, but it will keep them from controlling you.

**Two:** Speak plainly and truthfully, especially in small things. Integrity is built in ordinary conversations. Say what you mean and mean what you say — in your texts, emails, DMs, and commitments. Avoid vague promises or soft yeses that later become hard nos.

**Three:** When accused, resist the urge to retaliate. Focus on clarifying facts rather than attacking motives. Vindication belongs to the Lord. A soft answer does not mean weakness — it reflects confidence in God.

**Four:** Do not avoid difficult relationships if your conscience is clear. Avoidance often signals guilt, not wisdom. If you have walked uprightly, do not disappear when misunderstanding arises. Seek face-to-face conversation rather than letting suspicions grow.

**Five:** Be willing to call God as your witness. Living such a way that you can honestly say God knows your heart — and be comforted by that rather than frightened — is the goal. God sees beneath appearances and hears what others do not.

**Six:** Reflect the unchanging Christ in your reliability. Let your word be your bond. Christians should be known as dependable people. Psalm 15: we are people who keep our word even when it is inconvenient.

**Seven:** Examine the motives behind your decisions. Ask not only 'Is this permissible?' but 'Is this loving? Why am I doing this in this relationship?'

**Eight:** Speak hard truths with genuine love. Some people say: 'I'm just a truth teller.' But the Bible does not call you to be a truth teller — that is only half. You can use the truth to hurt people. The Bible says: be a truth-in-love teller. Check your heart before confronting: am I seeking their good or my relief? Am I seeking their good, or just wanting to justify myself?

**Nine:** Let love, not self-protection, shape your responses to your relationships. Paul's defense was never about protecting his reputation — it was about protecting people. When you find yourself in conflict and about to respond, ask yourself: am I defending myself or am I serving others?

**Ten:** Rest in God's final judgment. You do not need to win every argument to be faithful. God's verdict is the one that ultimately matters.

Paul shows us that when we are falsely accused, a clear conscience grounded in truth is our first defense — allowing us to respond calmly rather than defensively. And because we serve an unchanging and truthful Christ, our words, commitments, and attitudes reflect his faithfulness even when we are misunderstood. When truth is joined with genuine love for the brethren, God uses integrity and patience to expose false accusations and preserve the joy of his people.

Father in heaven, thank you that you are a good God. Thank you that you are a gracious God through Jesus Christ. Give us the grace to be the people that you call us to be. Help us to have a clear conscience. Help us to repent, confess, and receive your forgiveness whenever necessary. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
